comments.text: I don't think so? If $N$ works, then $N+1$ works as well, so there's no maximum.

I have a short preprint on arXiv: https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24139 proving some lower and upper bounds for this problem. For $k=3$, I prove the finite bound $$ g_3(n)\ge \frac{T_n-1}{2}+\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}T_j,\qquad T_m=[x^m](1+x+x^2)^m. $$ Thus $$ g_3(n)\ge \left(\frac{\sqrt3}{2\sqrt\pi}+o(1)\right)\frac{3^n}{\sqrt n}, $$ improving the earlier $3^n/n$ lower bound of Erdős and Sárközy. The proof uses the equivalence between 3AP-freeness of $H(A)$ and injectivity of all ternary sums $\sum_i\varepsilon_i a_i$, $\varepsilon_i\in{0,1,2}$, together with the exact bandwidth of the ternary grid. Note that it is also easy to get a lower bound of the order $\Omega(3^n/\sqrt{n})$ (with worse constant than the more involved argument above) with the classic second moment argument that analogously attains a $\Omega(2^n/\sqrt{n})$ bound for the distinct subset sum problem. This would already beat the Erdős and Sárközy $\Omega(3^n/n)$ bound. For fixed $k\ge4$, I prove $$ g_k(n)\gg_k \left(\frac{k-1}{k-2}\right)^n n^{-\log_2((k-1)/(k-2))}. $$ The local chain step in the proof also gives an improvement for Hilbert cubes in $k$-AP-free sets, replacing the Dietmann--Elsholtz growth base $k/(k-1)$ by $(k-1)/(k-2)$. The polynomial factor above comes from an additional averaging argument using the distinctness of the subset-sum generators. There is also a complementary construction using the standard idea of digit-restricted sets in base $p$ (for $p$ prime) showing $$ \limsup_{n\to\infty}g_k(n)^{1/n}\le \min_{p\ge3\ \mathrm{prime}}p^{2/(\min{p,k}-1)}. $$

Thanks, although I note that Erdős did not report what exponent they achieved in the $O(1)$ notation (in particular he never claimed a lower bound of $3^n/n$ as you say). It is entirely possible (although since this is unpublished we can not know for sure) that they were already aware of the bound $\gg 3^n/\sqrt{n}$ (indeed this seems likely, since Erdős was of course very familiar with the method that achieved $2^n/\sqrt{n}$ for [1], which basically works here also as you say). I believe that the real problem Erdős had in mind is whether $g_3(n)\gg 3^n$ (and so he probably didn't spend too much time thinking about how to optimise the constant).

Thank you for the response (and for running this wonderful site)! Understood; as you say, it's not surprising that Erdős would have been aware of the improved bounds and didn't take the time to optimize. My claim came from looking here: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0012365X9290119Z?via%3Dihub on pg. 261, where Erdős and Sárközy use the simple argument that the $3^n$ distinct ternary sums must occupy an interval of length $nN$. Hopefully my contribution of the general-$k$ bounds is still somewhat interesting!
